What does a real machine learning project actually look like? In this article, we’ll walk through an end-to-end machine learning workflow using a real customer churn prediction project.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Introduction
In the previous article, we explored the four main types of machine learning: supervised, unsupervised, semi-supervised, and reinforcement learning. We learned how each type differs based on the data available and the way the model learns.
Understanding these categories helps us choose the right approach for a problem, but it doesn’t answer another important question:
What does a real machine learning project actually look like?
Building a machine learning model involves much more than selecting an algorithm. Before a model can make predictions, we need to understand the problem, collect and prepare the data, train and evaluate the model, and finally deploy it for real-world use.
In this article, we’ll walk through the complete machine learning workflow using a telecom customer churn prediction project. Rather than looking at each step in isolation, we’ll see how they fit together to transform raw data into a model that can help a business make better decisions.
By the end of this article, you’ll understand the major phases of a machine learning project and how they connect to form a complete end-to-end workflow.

The Machine Learning Workflow at a Glance
Before we dive into each step, let’s look at the complete machine learning workflow.
Every machine learning project follows a series of stages, starting with a business problem and ending with predictions that help organizations make better decisions.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Machine Learning WorkflowAlthough the specific tools and algorithms may vary from one project to another, the overall workflow remains largely the same.
In this article, we’ll follow each of these steps using a real-world telecom customer churn prediction project.
What Happens at Each Step?
Let’s briefly understand what each stage accomplishes before exploring them in detail.

• Business Problem
Clearly identify the business problem you want to solve and determine whether machine learning is the right approach.

• Data Collection
Gather the historical data that will be used to train the machine learning model.

• Data Preparation
Clean, transform, and organize the data so it is ready for model training. This phase may include data cleaning, exploratory data analysis (EDA), feature engineering, and splitting the dataset.

• Modeling
Select an appropriate machine learning algorithm and train it using the prepared data.

• Evaluation
Measure the model's performance using unseen data to ensure it makes reliable predictions.

• Deployment & Monitoring
Deploy the trained model to a real-world environment, make predictions on new data, and continuously monitor its performance over time.

Our Example Project
To understand how these stages fit together, we’ll use a real-world machine learning project.
Imagine you’re working as a data scientist for a telecommunications company.
Every month, some customers cancel their subscriptions and switch to competitors. Since retaining existing customers is often less expensive than acquiring new ones, the company wants to identify customers who are likely to leave before they actually do.
Our goal is simple:
Can we predict whether a customer will churn?
Throughout this article, we’ll use this problem to explore each stage of the machine learning workflow.

Step 1: Business Problem
Every machine learning project begins with a business problem.
Our business problem is:
A telecommunications company is experiencing a high number of customers canceling their subscriptions. Customer churn leads to lost revenue, and acquiring new customers is often more expensive than retaining existing ones. The company wants to reduce churn by identifying at-risk customers early so it can take proactive retention actions, such as offering discounts or personalized promotions.
To help solve this business problem, we can formulate a machine learning problem:
Can we predict which customers are likely to churn before they leave?
This immediately tells us several things.
Input
Customer information

• Subscription length

• Frequency of use

• Age group

• …
Output
Will the customer churn?

• Yes

• No
Because the output is one of two categories, this is a supervised classification problem.
Customer Information
↓
Will Customer Churn?
Yes / No
Step 2: Data Collection
To train a machine learning model, we need historical data.
Fortunately, the telecom company already has customer records.
Our dataset is from an Iranian telecommunications company and it contains customer-level records collected over a 12-month period. It includes 3,150 observations, each representing an individual customer, with 13 attributes describing their usage patterns, service experience, and demographic characteristics.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Screenshot of the Dataset (csv)Notice the final column.
Churn is our label because we already know the correct answer for each customer.
The remaining columns are the features that the model will use to learn.
Why is Data Collection Important?
A machine learning model can only learn from the data it is given. If the collected data is incomplete, inaccurate, or unrepresentative, the model’s predictions will also be unreliable. Collecting high-quality, relevant data provides the foundation for building an accurate and effective machine learning model.

Step 3: Data Preparation
Once we’ve collected the data, it isn’t immediately ready for machine learning.
Real-world datasets often contain missing values, inconsistent formats, duplicate records, or irrelevant information. If we train a model using poor-quality data, the model will learn poor-quality patterns.
This is why data preparation is one of the most important stages of every machine learning project.
During this phase, data scientists transform raw data into a clean and structured dataset that is suitable for model training.
Some common tasks performed during data preparation include:

• Cleaning missing or incorrect values

• Removing duplicate records

• Converting categorical variables into numerical values

• Creating new features (feature engineering)

• Performing exploratory data analysis (EDA)

• Splitting the dataset into training and testing sets
Although these tasks may seem unrelated, they all have the same goal: to ensure the model learns from accurate and meaningful data.
For our telecom customer churn project, we prepared the dataset before training the model by:

• Exploring the data to understand customer characteristics and identify potential relationships with churn
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Example Plots from Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)
• Cleaning the data from missing and inconsistent values, removing dublicate records
# Example Code Block from Data Cleaning # check for dublicates
dub = df.duplicated().sum()
print("Dublicates:", dub)
# remove dublicates
df = df.drop_duplicates()
df.duplicated().sum()
df.shape

• Splitting the dataset into training and testing sets to evaluate the model fairly
# Example Code Block from Train/Test Splitfrom sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
# Features and target
X = df.drop('Churn', axis=1)
y = df['Churn']
# Train-test split (70/30, stratified to keep churn ratio)
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
X, y,
test_size=0.3,
random_state=42,
stratify=y
)
X_train.shape, X_test.shape
After completing these steps, the dataset was ready for machine learning.
Why is Data Preparation Important?
Raw data is rarely ready for machine learning. Cleaning, transforming, and organizing the data helps the model learn meaningful patterns instead of errors or inconsistencies. Well-prepared data leads to more accurate predictions and better overall model performance.

Step 4: Modeling
After the data has been prepared, it’s time to build the machine learning model.
This stage involves selecting an appropriate machine learning algorithm and training it using the prepared data. During training, the algorithm analyzes the training data and learns patterns that can be used to make predictions on new, unseen data.
The choice of algorithm depends on the problem being solved. For example, regression algorithms are used to predict numerical values, while classification algorithms are used to predict categories.
Since our goal is to determine whether a customer will churn or not churn, this is a binary classification problem.
For this project, we selected XGBoost, a powerful gradient boosting algorithm that is widely used in industry due to its strong predictive performance, ability to handle structured data, and robustness to missing values and complex relationships.
During training, the model learns how different customer characteristics influence the likelihood of churn. For example, it may discover that customers who complained more are more likely to leave.
The important point is that these relationships are not manually programmed. Instead, the algorithm discovers them automatically by learning from historical customer data.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Model Training DiagramNote: At this stage, you don’t need to understand how XGBoost works internally. The important idea is that a machine learning algorithm learns patterns from historical data to create a trained model. In future articles, we’ll explore different algorithms — including Decision Trees, Random Forests, and XGBoost — in detail.
Why is Modeling Important?
The model is the core of every machine learning system. It transforms historical data into knowledge that can be applied to future data.
However, a model is only as good as the data it learns from. Even the most advanced algorithm cannot compensate for poor-quality data. This is why data preparation and model training work hand in hand to produce reliable predictions.
In the next step, we’ll evaluate how well the model performs and determine whether its predictions are reliable enough for real-world use.

Step 5: Evaluation
Training a machine learning model is only part of the process. Before using it in a real-world application, we need to determine whether it makes reliable predictions.
This is the purpose of the evaluation stage.
During evaluation, the trained model is tested using the testing dataset, which contains customer records the model has never seen before. This allows us to measure how well the model generalizes to new data rather than simply memorizing the training examples.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Model Testing DiagramTo evaluate our customer churn model, we used several performance metrics including:

• Accuracy — How often the model makes the correct prediction.

• Precision — Of the customers predicted to churn, how many actually churned?

• Recall — Of the customers who actually churned, how many did the model identify?

• F1-Score — A balanced measure that combines precision and recall.

• ROC-AUC — Measures how well the model distinguishes between customers who churn and those who do not.
and Confusion Matrix — A table that summarizes the model’s predictions by comparing the predicted classes with the actual classes. It shows the numbers of true positives, true negatives, false positives, and false negatives, helping us understand the types of mistakes the model makes.
Confusion Matrix ExampleEach metric provides a different perspective on the model’s performance. Together, they help us determine whether the model is reliable enough for real-world use.
Why is Evaluation Important?
Imagine a student who scores 100% on the homework questions they practiced but struggles on the final exam. Although they appeared to perform well during practice, they didn’t truly understand the material.
